revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Jurōjin]][[de:Jurōjin]][[en:Jurōjin]][[es:Jurōjin]][[fa:جوروجین]][[fr:Jurōjin]][[id:Jurōjin]][[it:Jurōjin]][[ja:寿老人]][[pl:Jurōjin]][[ru:Дзюродзин]][[sh:Juroujin]][[simple:Juroujin]][[sv:Jurojin]][[tr:Jurōjin]][[vi:Jurōjin]][[zh:壽老人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Jurōjin]][[de:Jurōjin]][[en:Jurōjin]][[es:Jurōjin]][[fa:جوروجین]][[fr:Jurōjin]][[id:Jurōjin]][[it:Jurōjin]][[ja:寿老人]][[pl:Jurōjin]][[ru:Дзюродзин]][[sh:Juroujin]][[simple:Juroujin]][[sv:Jurojin]][[tr:Jurōjin]][[vi:Jurōjin]][[zh:壽老人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Jurōjin]][[de:Jurōjin]][[en:Jurōjin]][[es:Jurōjin]][[fa:جوروجین]][[fr:Jurōjin]][[id:Jurōjin]][[it:Jurōjin]][[ja:寿老人]][[pl:Jurōjin]][[ru:Дзюродзин]][[sh:Juroujin]][[simple:Juroujin]][[sv:Jurojin]][[tr:Jurōjin]][[vi:Jurōjin]][[zh:壽老人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Jurōjin]][[en:Jurōjin]][[es:Jurōjin]][[fa:جوروجین]][[fr:Jurōjin]][[id:Jurōjin]][[it:Jurōjin]][[ja:寿老人]][[pl:Jurōjin]][[ru:Дзюродзин]][[sh:Juroujin]][[simple:Juroujin]][[sv:Jurojin]][[tr:Jurōjin]][[vi:Jurōjin]][[zh:壽老人]]
{{short description|Taoist deity}} {{About|the Taoist god|the UK experimental rock band|Jurojin (band)}} [[File:Jurojin with deer.jpg|thumb|upright|Jurōjin with deer]] [[Image:Jurojin.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Japanese god of longevity]] [[File:Jurojin netsuke.JPG|thumb|upright|Jurojin. [[Netsuke]].]] In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, '''Jurōjin''' (寿老人) is one of the [[:en:Seven Gods of Fortune|Seven Gods of Fortune]] or ''Shichifukujin'', according to [[:en:Taoist|Taoist]] beliefs. He is the god of {{ill|Longevity|en|Longevity|qid=Q1066907|lt=Longevity|1=Longevity}}.<ref name="bocking" /><ref name="jaanus" /> Jurōjin originated from the Chinese [[:en:Taoist|Taoist]] god, the {{ill|Old Man of the South Pole|en|Old Man of the South Pole|qid=Q2200117|lt=Old Man of the South Pole|1=Old Man of the South Pole}}/Star of the Old Man. He is known as the immortal of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Northern Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} (960–1127), and may have been a historical figure of the period.<ref name="jaanus" /><ref name="d" /> Jurōjin is identified as the personification of the [[Pole star#Southern pole star (South Star)|Southern Polar Star]].<ref name="jaanus" /> While paintings and statues of Jurōjin are considered auspicious, he never developed a following independent of the other Seven Gods of Fortune.
People believe himself as the God of Wisdom and longevity had been live in the world for 1500 years. People respect to acquire safety and happy living.<ref>Schumacher, Mark. “God of Wisdom and Longevity.” Jurojin - Japanese Lucky God of Longevity, www.onmarkproductions.com/html/jurojin.shtml. </ref>
Jurōjin is often identified with [[Fukurokuju]], another of the Seven Gods of Fortune. In some accounts, the two are said to inhabit the same body.<ref name="graham" /> As such, the two are often confused.<ref name="n" />
Jurōjin walks with a staff and a [[:en:Fan (implement)|fan]]. He is depicted as an old man of slight stature, and by tradition, less than 3 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|qid=Q931805|lt=shaku|1=Shaku (unit)}} (approximately {{Convert|90|cm|in}}).<ref name="k" /> He is depicted with a long white beard and often a very tall, bald head.<ref name="n" /> He has a scroll tied to his staff, on which is written the lifespan of all living things. The scroll is sometimes identified as a {{ill|Buddhist texts|en|Buddhist texts|qid=Q1001051|lt=Buddhist sutra|1=Buddhist texts}}. The {{ill|Deer|en|Deer|qid=Q23390|lt=Deer|1=Deer}}, a symbol of longevity, usually (but not always) accompanies him as a messenger, as do other long-lived animals such as the {{ill|Crane (bird)|en|Crane (bird)|qid=Q25365|lt=crane|1=Crane (bird)}} and the {{ill|Tortoise|en|Tortoise|qid=Q46360|lt=Tortoise|1=Tortoise}}.<ref name="k" />
Jurōjin is a popular subject of Japanese {{ill|Ink wash painting|en|Ink wash painting|qid=Q756712|lt=Ink wash painting|1=Ink wash painting}}s. He was introduced into the Japanese art tradition by {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} Buddhist painters, and depictions of Jurōjin span from the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1337–1573) through the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603–1868). Artists who depicted Jurōjin as a subject include {{ill|Sesshū Tōyō|en|Sesshū Tōyō|qid=Q48514|lt=Sesshū|1=Sesshū Tōyō}} (1420–1506), {{ill|Sesson Shukei|en|Sesson Shukei|qid=Q3480606|lt=Sesson Shukei|1=Sesson Shukei}} (1504–1589), {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|qid=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū|1=Kanō Tan'yū}} (1602–1674), and {{ill|Maruyama Ōkyo|en|Maruyama Ōkyo|qid=Q405444|lt=Maruyama Ōkyo|1=Maruyama Ōkyo}} (1733–1795).<ref name="jaanus" />
==See also== *[[:en:Fu, Lu, and Shou|Fu, Lu, and Shou]] *{{ill|Old Man of the South Pole|en|Old Man of the South Pole|qid=Q2200117|lt=Old Man of the South Pole|1=Old Man of the South Pole}} *[[Fukurokuju]]
==References== {{Commonscat|Jurōjin}} {{reflist|2|refs=
<ref name="bocking">{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shintō|year=1997|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=0-203-98627-X|pages=62|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y0TIfrZuq_YC&pg=PA62}}</ref>
<ref name="d">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru Daijisen |title=寿老人 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |access-date=2013-01-23 |year=2013 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |trans-title=Jurōjin |oclc=56431036 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archive-date=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
<ref name="graham">{{cite book|last=Graham|first=Patricia|title=Faith and Power in Japanese Buddhist Art, 1600-2005|year=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3126-4|pages=110|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EBABpv4uXYgC&pg=PA110}}</ref>
<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web | url = http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/j/juroujin.htm | title = Juroujin 寿老人 | date = 2001 | publisher = JAANUS: Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System | location = Tokyo, Japan | access-date = 2013-01-23}}</ref>
<ref name="k">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten |title=寿老人 |trans-title=Jurōjin |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |access-date=2012-12-14 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |oclc=56431036 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archive-date=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
<ref name="n">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) |title=寿老人 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |access-date=2013-01-12 |year=2013 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |trans-title=Jurōjin |oclc=153301537 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archive-date=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
}}
